prompt_md: Why can't `operator<<` itself be a virtual member function in a class hierarchy?

options.a: Operator overloads are always resolved at compile time and cannot be virtual.
options.b: The left operand of `<<` is `std::ostream&`, so the function must be a non-member; only member functions can be virtual.
options.c: Making `operator<<` virtual would require the stream to have a vtable.
options.d: Virtual operator overloads are allowed but are not inherited by derived classes.

explanation_md: For `std::cout << obj` to work, the `operator<<` must have `std::ostream&` as its left operand. That means it must be a non-member (or a member of `std::ostream`, which you cannot modify). Only member functions can be declared `virtual`, so a non-member `operator<<` cannot be virtual. The workaround is to have it call a virtual member function such as `print`.

`operator<<` takes `const Base&`, which binds to `c` without slicing. Inside it calls `b.print(out)`. Because `print` is virtual and `b` actually refers to a `Child`, dispatch goes to `Child::print`, which outputs `"Child"`.

`operator<<` calls `l.print(out)` via virtual dispatch, landing in `Leaf::print`. That function first calls `Node::print(out)` (the base version), which writes `Node`. Then it writes `+Leaf`. The total output on one line is `Node+Leaf`.

prompt_md: The `operator<<` for a `Shape` hierarchy is defined in `Shape` as a `friend` that calls `shape.print(out)`. A new class `Triangle : public Shape` is added. What does `Triangle` need to do to support `std::cout << myTriangle`?

options.a: Define its own `friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream&, const Triangle&)`.
options.c: Nothing — `Triangle` automatically inherits `operator<<` including the `Shape` output.
options.d: Declare `operator<<` as a virtual member of `Triangle`.

explanation_md: The single `operator<<` in `Shape` works for the entire hierarchy because it calls the virtual `print`. `Triangle` only needs to override `print` to customize its output. No new `operator<<` is needed. Option (c) is wrong because without an override, `Triangle` would print as a `Shape`.
